candidate_problems.text: Can 'record-forming capacity' be defined non-circularly and mathematically enough to derive the Born rule uniquely, rather than merely redescribing amplitude-squared in computational language? The key tension is whether persistence, reproducibility, compositional consistency, and redundant encoding independently constrain the measure, or whether the conjecture smuggles in Born weighting through those notions.

What exact dynamical link connects quantum amplitude to the emergence of durable, redundantly encoded records across decoherence and repeated interactions? The open question is whether one can show from unitary dynamics plus a theory of record stability that branches with larger amplitude-squared systematically generate more accessible downstream memory, instead of this being an analogy to evolutionary selection without a worked mechanism.

How observer- and coarse-graining-independent is the proposed measure over 'distributed computational memory'? The unresolved tension is whether record counts, redundancy, and branch identity can be made basis-invariant and decomposition-invariant across different descriptions of the same quantum process, since without that the proposal may inherit the same arbitrariness that defeats naive branch counting.
